prompt: Which author wrote the Harry Potter series?
What is the name of the whale in Moby-Dick?
In "Romeo and Juliet", the two feuding families are the Montagues and the…?
Which Shakespeare play features the line "To be, or not to be"?
The opening line "It was the best of times, it was the worst of times" belongs to which novel?
What is the word for the dot above the letters 'i' and 'j'? It's the smallest named part of a letter.
George Orwell chose "1984" as the title by inverting the year he finished writing the book. What year did he actually finish it?
A pangram is a sentence using every letter of the alphabet at least once. Which of these is the most famous English pangram?
options: Sphinx of black quartz, judge my vow.
Pack my box with five dozen liquor jugs.
The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
How vexingly quick daft zebras jump!

prompt: Mary Shelley wrote Frankenstein after a famous bet with friends to each write a ghost story. Which famous poet hosted the gathering?
The word "salary" comes from the Latin "salarium". What were Roman soldiers reportedly paid — at least partially — in, giving the word its root?
Which novel, famously written without using the letter 'e', is the longest lipogrammatic novel in English literature?
options: Ella Minnow Pea by Mark Dunn

prompt: The first known use of the word "selfie" in print appeared in an online post from which country in 2002?
In "Charlotte's Web", what kind of animal is Charlotte?
Which detective lives at 221B Baker Street?
What is a group of related books, told in order, usually called?
In Roald Dahl's story, which golden item lets children tour Willy Wonka's factory?
What do you call the short summary printed on the back of a book to tempt you to read it?
Which long-haired princess in a Grimm fairy tale lets down her hair from a tower?
A word that means the opposite of another word is called what?
Which author created the world of Middle-earth, the setting of The Hobbit?
Dr. Seuss wrote "Green Eggs and Ham" to win a bet from his publisher. What was the dare?
options: Use no more than 50 different words

prompt: What does the common abbreviation "e.g." actually stand for in Latin?
In Greek mythology and Homer's epics, whose 'heel' became a phrase for a fatal weakness?
Jane Austen's most famous novel opens by declaring a single rich man "must be in want of" what?
A 'haiku' is a short poem traditionally built on a syllable pattern across three lines. What is that pattern?
The word "nightmare" originally referred to a 'mare' that was believed to be what?
options: A demon that sat on sleepers

prompt: An 'oxymoron' joins two contradictory words. Which of these phrases is a true oxymoron?
Which Gabriel García Márquez novel opens with Colonel Aureliano Buendía facing a firing squad?
options: Love in the Time of Cholera

prompt: The everyday word "quarantine" comes from an Italian phrase meaning what length of time ships once waited at port?
In classic poetry, a line of five 'iambic' feet — the rhythm Shakespeare wrote in — is called what?
The word "robot" entered English from a 1920 Czech play by Karel Čapek. What does its root word mean?
In Lewis Carroll's books, which young girl falls down a rabbit hole into Wonderland?
What do you call a made-up name an author uses instead of their real one?
Which hungry insect eats its way through a week of food in Eric Carle's classic picture book?
In the classic nursery rhyme, who sat on a wall, had a great fall, and could not be put back together?
In Pinocchio, what part of the puppet's body grows longer whenever he tells a lie?
A word that sounds like the noise it describes — such as 'buzz', 'sizzle', or 'boom' — is an example of what?
Which two words, spelled the same backwards and forwards, are examples of a palindrome?
Bram Stoker's "Dracula" is told entirely through letters, diary entries, and newspaper clippings. What is this storytelling style called?
Agatha Christie is one of the best-selling novelists of all time, but she also wrote romance novels under which pen name?
The famous comma that comes before 'and' in a list — as in 'red, white, and blue' — is commonly named after which university?
Many consider Cervantes' "Don Quixote" the first modern novel. What does the deluded hero famously mistake windmills for?
The word "serendipity" was coined by writer Horace Walpole, inspired by a fairy tale about princes from 'Serendip' — an old name for which country?
Which of these is a real, recognised collective noun for a group of crows?
Roald Dahl invented a whole vocabulary of nonsense words for his giant in "The BFG". What did the BFG call the fizzy drink whose bubbles go downwards?
The novel "Frankenstein" carries a famous subtitle linking it to a figure from Greek myth who stole fire. What is that subtitle?
F. Scott Fitzgerald nearly published "The Great Gatsby" under a very different title. Which of these was one of his seriously considered choices?
The English words "chortle" and "galumph" were both invented by Lewis Carroll in a single nonsense poem. Which poem?
Samuel Richardson's 1740 novel "Pamela" was so popular it spawned mocking parodies — one of which grew into a famous novel of its own. Which author wrote that response, "Shamela"?
The Brontë sisters first published under male-sounding pen names that kept their own initials. Which set of names did Charlotte, Emily, and Anne use?
The everyday word "tantalize" comes from the Greek myth of Tantalus, who was punished in the underworld in what specific way?
options: Filling a leaking jar with water for eternity
Having his liver eaten daily by an eagle
Food and water staying just out of reach
Pushing a boulder forever up a hill
